skill.name: minimax-xlsx
skill.description: Create, inspect, edit, format, and validate Excel and spreadsheet files using OOXML-focused helper scripts and spreadsheet analysis guides.
skill.summary: Create, edit, analyze, and validate Excel workbooks with OOXML-aware workflows.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts, but most are false positives from markdown command examples, OOXML schema URLs, formula text, and spreadsheet terminology. Confirmed risk remains medium because the skill uses local helper scripts, rewrites workbook archives, and can invoke LibreOffice headless on user-supplied spreadsheets.

medium_findings.title: Headless LibreOffice Execution on User Workbooks
medium_findings.description: The recalculation helper discovers LibreOffice and runs it on user-provided spreadsheet files. Arguments are passed without a shell and include timeouts, so command injection is not evident, but processing untrusted Office files through a large external application increases local execution and denial-of-service exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct subprocess.run calls are present and semantically used for workbook recalculation. The risk is bounded because shell=True is not used and the command target is a discovered LibreOffice binary.

medium_findings.title: Archive Unpack and Workbook Rewrite Workflow
medium_findings.description: The unpack helper extracts user-supplied XLSX archives and rewrites XML files for editing. It validates archive member paths before extraction, which mitigates Zip Slip, but the workflow still deletes and recreates output directories and writes many files based on workbook content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The archive extraction and file rewrite behavior is explicit in the helper scripts. The path traversal check substantially reduces exploitability, so this is a medium operational risk rather than a confirmed vulnerability.

medium_findings.title: Script Chaining for Row Insertion
medium_findings.description: The row insertion helper starts another local helper script through subprocess.run. It uses an argument array and sys.executable rather than a shell, but it still executes local code using user-selected workbook paths and row parameters.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is confirmed and has a legitimate purpose. Injection evidence is weak because the command does not invoke a shell and the helper path is derived from the script directory.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Mostly Occur in Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts point to markdown command examples that teach users how to run helper scripts. These examples can lead an agent to execute commands during normal use, but they are not hidden code execution inside the marketplace package itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review showed shell commands inside markdown workflows and installation guidance. They are user-visible operational instructions, not obfuscated or automatically executed payloads.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are OOXML Namespaces and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are mainly XML namespace identifiers, relationship type URIs, or reference links. No evidence found of requests, sockets, fetch calls, or outbound data transfer in the Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs are OOXML identifiers and documentation references. A repository search found no Python HTTP client usage or network execution path.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Spreadsheet Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: C2-style alerts align with Excel cell references such as C2 and formula examples, while weak-crypto alerts align with words such as hash, key, or MD5-like text in spreadsheet documentation. No evidence found of command-and-control behavior or cryptographic routines used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious terms are explainable by spreadsheet formulas, cell addresses, and OOXML concepts. No matching exfiltration, persistence, or cryptographic misuse pattern was found in the reviewed executable scripts.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run for External Application Launch
dangerous_patterns.description: LibreOffice is launched with subprocess.run to recalculate formulas in a user-supplied workbook.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The call is direct and confirmed. It is controlled by an argument list, so the concern is external file processing rather than shell injection.

dangerous_patterns.title: ZIP Archive Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: XLSX files are extracted with zipfile.extractall after validating member paths for traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Archive extraction is confirmed and security-relevant. The implementation includes a path traversal guard, reducing severity.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet edits can break formulas, styles, and workbook structure. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guided workflows for creating, editing, and validating XLSX files.
actual_capabilities: Reads XLSX, XLSM, CSV, and TSV files for structure, preview data, data quality issues, and numeric summaries.
Creates new XLSX workbooks from a minimal OOXML template with sheets, styles, formulas, and shared strings.
Edits existing XLSX workbooks by unpacking XML, preserving workbook structure, and repacking the result.
Adds columns and inserts rows while copying styles and updating relevant formulas.
Checks formulas for error values, missing references, broken named ranges, and shared formula integrity.
Optionally uses LibreOffice headless to recalculate formula caches when LibreOffice is installed.
limitations: Legacy .xls files are not supported and must be converted to .xlsx first.
Dynamic formula recalculation depends on a local LibreOffice installation.
Complex workbook features such as macros, pivots, charts, and VBA require careful preservation checks.
The helpers operate on local files and temporary directories, so users should avoid untrusted spreadsheets.

use_cases.title: Build a Financial Workbook
use_cases.description: Create a multi-sheet model with input assumptions, formulas, styles, totals, and validation checks.

use_cases.title: Repair a Spreadsheet Deliverable
use_cases.description: Inspect an existing workbook, fix formula references, add rows or columns, and preserve original sheets.

use_cases.title: Audit Tabular Data Quality
use_cases.description: Read Excel, CSV, or TSV files and report missing values, duplicate rows, mixed types, and summaries.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Workbook
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis of a spreadsheet file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Open the attached workbook, list the sheets, summarize the columns, and report any data quality issues.

prompt_templates.title: Create a New Model
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate a new XLSX file from requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook with assumptions, calculations, and summary sheets. Use formulas for all derived values and validate it before delivery.

prompt_templates.title: Edit Without Format Loss
prompt_templates.scenario: Modify an existing XLSX workbook while preserving structure.
prompt_templates.prompt: Update the existing workbook by inserting the requested row, copying nearby styles, updating formulas, and verifying the original sheets remain intact.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Recalculate
prompt_templates.prompt: Run static formula validation, use LibreOffice recalculation if available, then summarize formula errors, skipped checks, and recommended fixes.

output_examples.input: Analyze a quarterly sales workbook for data quality issues.
output_examples.output: A concise workbook report with sheet names, row counts, column types, missing values, duplicates, and numeric summaries.

output_examples.input: Add a percentage-of-total column to an existing budget workbook.
output_examples.output: An updated XLSX file with the new column, copied styles, formulas, total row formatting, and validation notes.

output_examples.input: Create a new three-sheet financial model.
output_examples.output: A formatted workbook containing assumptions, calculations, summary outputs, live formulas, and a formula validation result.

best_practices: Keep a backup of the original workbook before editing any existing XLSX file.
Validate formulas after every create, edit, repair, or recalculation workflow.
Use blue font for hardcoded inputs, black for formulas, and green for cross-sheet references.
anti_patterns: Do not use openpyxl round trips for complex existing workbooks with macros, pivots, or advanced formatting.
Do not hardcode calculated values when an Excel formula should keep the workbook live.
Do not trust row numbers from a prompt without checking the actual worksheet labels first.

faq.question: Can this skill edit existing Excel files?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses an unpack, XML edit, and repack workflow designed to preserve workbook structure.

faq.question: Does it support CSV and TSV files?
faq.answer: Yes. The reader can load CSV and TSV files for analysis, summaries, and quality checks.

faq.question: Can it evaluate Excel formulas?
faq.answer: It can statically detect formula errors and can use LibreOffice for dynamic recalculation when available.

faq.question: Will it preserve macros and pivot tables?
faq.answer: The workflow is designed to avoid unnecessary workbook rewrites, but complex features still require careful verification.

faq.question: Why does it edit OOXML directly?
faq.answer: Direct OOXML edits reduce format loss for existing XLSX files and allow precise formula and style changes.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted spreadsheets?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill handles archives and may launch LibreOffice, so untrusted files should be reviewed in a sandbox.
